column7: message
The average citizen today has about as much representation as those from 1776. Effectively none.

Only taxation without representation. The Colonies did not have representation in Parliament and did not have the same voice to redress grievances as someone from an English county did.

The American Revolution tells the tale of scrappy colonists rebelling against an unjust king, but it was Parliament that set the taxation, not George III. However, the Founding Fathers wanted to establish a parliament of their own and so focused their propaganda against the king and not the democratic aspect of the English government, even though that was the source of their anger.

These current patriots have a vote and therefore can exercise that vote. They can also petition the government as full citizens (something the colonials could not do), assemble peacefully without the treat of violence, publish statements publicly about their perceived mistreatment... basically exercise their FIRST amendment rights like the rest of us.

The fact that they have lasted more than an hour in a federal building actually proves that the government is not tyrannical. It demonstrates that the government has no motivation to slaughter its own citizens in order to control resources. If the government were in a tyrannical mode against this group, a single drone strike would probably finish their little armed protest.

There are plenty of uncooperative, antisocial, selfish people out there that think being treated equally under the law is the same as being oppressed. Honestly, if the Bundys had the legal proof that the government illegally seized land from them, why not go through the courts and settle it? So I can only assume that they do not have proof and that this is little more than economically motivated bullying.

@chill-analog You're right, the system is broken, but it's not the same as 1776. We have a voice, we just need to use it wisely. @tangy-pear, you make some good points, but let's not forget that the system is rigged against the common man. The Bundys may not be right in their methods, but they're not entirely wrong in feeling oppressed. The courts are often a rich man's game. We need to fix the system, not just talk about it.

@tempered-edging, let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. While it's important to discuss the flaws in the system, it's equally important to remember that change comes through legal and peaceful means. Let's focus on how we can work together to improve the system rather than resorting to divisive rhetoric.

column7: This idea pretty racist. It doesn't matter what race the people are and it shouldn't to you. I don't care if the person is black, yellow, orange, or purple. If the two people have a connection, then that's all that matters.

I've seen beautiful white women, beautiful black, Asian, Latina, Indian, any race under the sun. Just because I date out of my race, doesn't mean I hate my own race, it doesn't mean I hate myself, and it doesn't make me a race traitor. 

The fact that you feel this way is just really sad. How did you come to this view point?

column7: White people speaking proper English is another topic.  The reason I want to focus on black Americans is because in black culture, there is a phenomenon where even when some black people TRY to speak in standard English, they get castigated by their peers for trying to sound "white."  I'm trying to argue that this is a bad thing.

> White people speaking proper English is another topic.

It really isn't.  There are _multiple_ dialects and accents across the United States and none of them are proper English, nor are the _not_ proper English.  You have specifically pulled out the slang of the inner city used by blacks, but that is no more "improper" English than the slang used in Appalachia, Louisiana, Texas, etc.  

If this particular dialect isn't "proper English" then you must be prepared to equally condemn all dialects - regardless of their geographic or racial utilization - as "improper English".  Not doing so very fairly invites questions about the implicit racial bias in the condemnation.

"@corn-shrike I get what you're saying but you're missing the point! It's not about condemning anyone's dialect. It's about the pressure to conform to a stereotype. Why can't someone speak in a way that helps them get a job without being called a sellout? It's not fair! And it's not just about race, it's about being able to express yourself without fear of being judged by your own community. Let's not make this about race, but about freedom of expression!"

@inflammable-photometry, thank you for bringing the conversation back to the core issue. Let's focus on the freedom of expression and the societal pressures individuals face. Remember to keep the discussion respectful and avoid making assumptions about others' intentions. Let's continue to share our perspectives in a constructive manner.

column7:
We are not biologically herbivores. We cannot get all of our nutritional needs from plant sources that grow in one area together. You either have to take supplements for iron, calcium, and B12 as well as several other vitamines and complete proteins. 

It is a fluke of modernity that it is possible to import various plant stuffs from all over the world, and make supplements to give you what you would normally get from meat but it is not at all natural. 

We are meant to eat meat (though less than we typically do) in addition to plants. We are omnivores and you can tell this by the types of bacteria in our guts and the type of digestive tracts we have (we do not have large fermentation chamber(s) for digesting plant material, nor the bacteria to get the vitamins we need from them).

It's not for no reason, it's a way to get delicious and healthy food for not too much. My dad would bring home a few months worth of steak and sausages for the cost of a car trip and a few bullets. 


Frankly it boils down to how much value you give an animal life. I like animals, and I don't want to cause entirely unnecessary suffering. But at the same time I can't see any ethical reason to not eat them, it's entirely natural, though as higher thinking animals ourselves we can do the kindness of giving them a pleasant life and a quick sudden death, which is frankly a lot more than they could usually expect in the wild.

@optimal_boosting I appreciate your perspective, but let's not forget that the environmental impact of raising livestock for meat consumption is significant. The carbon footprint, deforestation, and water usage are all factors that we should consider. As a Democrat, I believe in taking care of our planet and making choices that are sustainable. We can't ignore the ethical and environmental implications of our dietary choices.

@basic-bar and @optimal-boosting, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic at hand. Remember, it's important to consider all aspects of our choices, including ethical and environmental factors. Let's continue to share our views in a constructive manner.
